nativeRange.habitat: Sheltered coral reefs with rich coral growth at depths of roughly 5–30 m; tends to congregate in small groups among branching corals and rocky crevices

diet.notes: Has been documented accepting a varied carnivorous diet in captivity, including mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped meaty foods (krill, shrimp, squid, clam, mussel), and most quality marine flake or pellet preparations. Generally advised to feed 2–3 small meals per day.

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Mrbrefast; 3648x2736. Sharp full-body side profile showing the diagnostic vivid yellow body and bold magenta-purple crown stripe running from the snout over the eye along the dorsal ridge; well-lit against live rock.

Photo by Bernard DUPONT; 4416x2649. Correctly-ID'd individual in natural reef habitat showing yellow body and purple dorsal stripe; subject smaller in frame among other reef fish, kept as habitat-context extra.

Photo by Rickard Zerpe; 1239x929. Sharp, vividly-coloured head-on close-up against a red sponge showing purple crown and yellow face; tight crop cutting off the body, kept as non-recommended detail shot.

summary: A small, vivid yellow-and-purple Western Pacific dottyback that is generally considered beginner-friendly and reef-safe. Best kept singly, as it tends to be territorial and has been known to eat ornamental shrimp.
careNotes:
The Purple Stripe Dottyback, also widely traded under the older name Pseudochromis diadema, is generally considered one of the more straightforward saltwater fish for new keepers — it tends to be hardy, eats readily, and tolerates a typical reef-tank parameter window. It spends most of its time darting in and out of rockwork in the mid water column, so plenty of caves, overhangs, and branching structure are generally advised.

Temperament reports vary across sources, ranging from peaceful to aggressive; in practice the species tends to defend a small home territory in the rockwork and has been known to bully smaller, more timid fish. Moderately robust tankmates — tangs, larger damselfish, hawkfish, or angelfish — are generally a better fit than shy gobies or anthias.

Conspecific aggression is reported as high — keeping one per tank is the standard recommendation unless the system is unusually large with multiple discrete territories. Reef compatibility is generally considered good with corals and sessile invertebrates, but the species has been known to pick at small ornamental shrimp and other mobile micro-invertebrates, so a dedicated shrimp-and-fish reef is not the safest pairing.

The species was reclassified from Pseudochromis to Pictichromis, and the accepted scientific name is now Pictichromis diadema. Most retail listings still use the older Pseudochromis diadema name.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to spawn in captivity. The mature male typically prepares a cave or crevice and courts a female who deposits a small egg ball inside the prepared site. Captive-bred specimens are increasingly available in the trade and tend to be more even-tempered than wild-caught stock. Raising fry is generally considered moderately challenging due to the small size of the larvae.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Used for minimum tank size (30 gal), water parameters (temp 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), care level (Easy), temperament (Aggressive), reef-safe (Yes), diet (carnivore — meaty foods), max size (3 in), and the caveat about eating ornamental shrimp. Note: LiveAquaria still lists the older synonym Pseudochromis diadema.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked: scientific name (Pseudochromis diadema synonym), 30 gal minimum, 2.5 in adult size, aggressive temperament, beginner care level, carnivore diet, reef-safe.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for confirmed accepted scientific name Pictichromis diadema, family Pseudochromidae, order Ovalentaria, max length 6.2 cm TL, depth range 5–30 m, native distribution (Philippines, Peninsular Malaysia, northern Borneo), reef-associated habitat.

additional.name: Reefarchive
additional.notes: Third source for water parameters (72–80°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.026, dKH 8–12), care level (beginner), reef-safe (yes), lifespan (5–7 years), and conflicting temperament report (peaceful) used to balance the LiveAquaria/BlueZoo aggressive rating to semi-aggressive. Adult size 2.4 in (6.1 cm).

fish.breedingNotes: Cave-spawning species; male prepares a hiding site and courts the female, who deposits a small egg ball inside the cave. Captive-bred specimens are increasingly available and tend to be more even-tempered than wild-caught fish. Raising fry is considered moderately challenging.
